title: ‘Eternals’ Is a Big Miss. But Is Marvel Too Big to Fail?
format: Sean Fennessey and Joanna Robinson dissect why Eternals misfires and ask whether Marvel is too big to fail, then Sean talks to Joanna Hogg about The Souvenir Part II.

segments.heading: ‘Eternals’ and whether Marvel is too big to fail

films.title: Eternals
films.note: Ten new immortals, millennia of history, tones shifting the whole way through. Skilled people, bungled execution — the rare MCU film that feels like it got away from them. Zhao's naturalist eye buried under mediocre CGI demon dogs; the story holds its cards too close for too long.

films.title: Guardians of the Galaxy
films.note: The counter-example: a riskier cosmic bet that paid off because it stayed a found-family character story instead of skipping around in time.

films.title: Thor: Ragnarok
films.note: The most Kirby-coded MCU film — big electric colors, gonzo space. Proof world-hopping works when you're already invested in the characters.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: Zhao's Oscar winner; the naturalism that made hiring her make sense, and that Marvel then glopped CGI over.

films.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
films.note: Robinson's example of a recent MCU film that landed — villain included.

films.title: Captain Marvel
films.note: The 'designed by committee' precedent: Boden and Fleck handed the acting on Earth while Marvel made everything else. Still a coherent film; this one isn't.

films.title: The Incredible Hulk
films.note: The last time a star seized the edit — Edward Norton — before Zhao's insistence on her own cut. Feige's most censorious comment ever came out of it.

films.title: American History X
films.note: Norton reportedly took this edit over too; director Tony Kaye wanted his name off it.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: The series finale Marvel has been working past ever since — the 'okay, now what' problem.

films.title: Avengers: Age of Ultron
films.note: The go-to example of a film bogged down by the burden of setting up what comes next.

films.title: The Green Knight
films.note: Keoghan's idiosyncratic, mumbly register carries straight over into his Eternals performance.

films.title: Yesterday
films.note: Robinson's parallel horror — its living-John-Lennon coda struck her the way the film's Hiroshima beat struck Sean.

films.title: The Souvenir Part II
films.note: The second half of Hogg's autobiographical diptych — Julie reconstructing the film she just lived through. A Russian doll of a self, in a different musical key from Part I.

films.title: The Souvenir
films.note: Part I; Hogg made the decision not to read any response to it before shooting Part II.

films.title: Ticket of No Return
films.note: The Ulrike Ottinger film she saw at the Scala in London before film school that made her want to direct.

films.title: Day for Night
films.note: Rewatched as a seminal film about filmmaking.

films.title: 8½
films.note: The other touchstone she returned to for films about making films.

films.title: Heat
films.note: Her last-great pick — Michael Mann's film as the ultimate LA portrait, ambitious in a way she'd love to attempt but doubts she could.

films.title: Straight Time
films.note: Ulu Grosbard's Dustin Hoffman film, just out on Blu-ray; she calls the performances phenomenal.

films: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness

Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Ring.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings

Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madn.title: Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness
